hot.title: Run /taxonomize-align-code
hot.body:
Goal:
Align Go identifiers (types, functions, vars, fields, comments, error
messages) to TAXONOMY.md. Produces a rename plan, gates on review, then
executes language-aware refactors.

Verification:
1. A rename plan is committed at `tasks/reports/K-04-rename-plan.md` and approved before any rename runs.
2. After execution, all type names match canonical taxonomy: `manifest`, `appManifest`, `envManifest`, `store`, `secretEntry`, `namespaceView`, `webServer` — confirm or rename per TAXONOMY.md.
3. All exported and package-internal identifiers in main.go are consistent with TAXONOMY.md (e.g. if `namespace` is canonical, `appEnv` becomes `namespace`).
4. `go build ./...` and `go test ./...` both pass with no behavioral change.
5. Public CLI surface is unchanged: `thimble init`, `set`, `render`, etc. — user-visible verbs are sticky and only renamed if TAXONOMY.md explicitly marks them as drift.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-04; wave/step 0.4; effort M; risk med
- Files: cmd/thimble/main.go, cmd/thimble/main_test.go
- Depends on: K-03
- Notes: This is the last knot in Wave 0. After it lands, Wave 2's package split has
clean naming to organize against. Do not allow this knot to touch CLI verbs or
flag names without a paired README/CHANGELOG entry.

hot.title: Run /taxonomize-align-docs
hot.body:
Goal:
Apply TAXONOMY.md to existing markdown. Replace synonyms with canonical terms,
flag contradictions, and produce a unified voice across all three docs.

Verification:
1. README.md, SECURITY.md, and thimble.md all use canonical terminology from TAXONOMY.md exclusively.
2. The skill's diff is reviewed and committed; no new content added — only rewording for term alignment.
3. Any contradictions the skill could not auto-resolve are listed in `tasks/reports/K-03-doc-align-conflicts.md` for human resolution.
4. A grep for legacy synonyms (e.g. "store", "vault", "envfile") returns no hits except inside fenced code blocks where removal would break the example.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-03; wave/step 0.3; effort S; risk low
- Files: README.md, SECURITY.md, thimble.md
- Depends on: K-02
- Notes: The README's "Real-Life Flow" deliberately uses concrete sample names
(`web-api`, `production`); leave those alone. Only realign nouns/verbs that are
part of Thimble's vocabulary, not user-supplied identifiers.

hot.title: Run /taxonomize
hot.body:
Goal:
Extract the canonical Thimble vocabulary — `app`, `environment`, `namespace`,
`recipient`, `bundle`, `manifest`, `identity`, `render`, `provision`, `set`,
`and-set`, `and-get`, `peer`, `operator`, `deploy host`, `recovery recipient`
— into a single `TAXONOMY.md`. Surface terminology drift between
[README.md](README.md), [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md), [thimble.md](thimble.md),
and [cmd/thimble/main.go](cmd/thimble/main.go) so later waves can resolve it.

Verification:
1. `TAXONOMY.md` exists at repo root with: nouns, verbs, phrases, and per-term definition + canonical synonym (e.g. "namespace = `<app>/<env>`").
2. The skill's drift report is committed at `tasks/reports/K-02-taxonomy.md` listing every term that is used inconsistently across docs and code.
3. No source/doc edits yet; this knot only catalogs.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-02; wave/step 0.2; effort S; risk low
- Files: TAXONOMY.md (new)
- Depends on: K-01
- Notes: Thimble overloads `set` (CLI verb), `set` (Go method), and "set" as in "the
secret has been set" — that's exactly the kind of ambiguity TAXONOMY.md should
freeze. Same with `environment` (deploy context) vs `env` (manifest field) vs
`env` (CLI argument).

hot.title: Run /enforce-sourcecode-size
hot.body:
Goal:
Establish file/function/line-length budgets for the repo before any other work
begins. Today `cmd/thimble/main.go` is 1,394 lines in a single file; that's the
strongest signal that there are no agreed limits. Run `/enforce-sourcecode-size`
to produce concrete budgets and a delta report.

Verification:
1. A budget file (e.g. `.sourcecode-size.toml` or whatever the skill emits) is committed at repo root.
2. The skill's report is captured in `tasks/reports/K-01-enforce.md` and lists each over-budget file/function with its measured size.
3. README "Contributing" pointer (or CONTRIBUTING.md after K-07) references the budget file.
4. Repository builds and tests still pass; no source modifications yet.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-01; wave/step 0.1; effort S; risk low
- Files: cmd/thimble/main.go, cmd/thimble/main_test.go, any new size-budget config
- Notes: This knot only measures and ratifies budgets. It does not split files — that's
K-12. Treat the budget as the contract Wave 2 must satisfy.

hot.title: Thimble hardening plan
hot.body: Parent execution_plan for the 48-knot Thimble hardening rollout. See tasks/knot-plan.md for the human-readable map and tasks/knots/K-NN-*.md for individual knot drafts.
hot.description: Parent execution_plan for the 48-knot Thimble hardening rollout. See tasks/knot-plan.md for the human-readable map and tasks/knots/K-NN-*.md for individual knot drafts.

execution_plan_data.objective: Take Thimble from v0.0 hobby to a small-team-trustable secrets manager: source shape, vocabulary, legal/trust scaffolding, code restructure, CI gates, runtime hardening, web UI hardening, recipient governance, release & install hardening, DevX polish.

waves.name: Wave 1: Foundation Alignment
waves.objective: Source-shape and vocabulary baseline before any other work begins

waves.name: Wave 2: Legal & Trust Scaffolding
waves.objective: Repo passes basic procurement smell-test; trust enablers in place

waves.name: Wave 3: Code Structure
waves.objective: main.go split into auditable packages with clear trust boundaries

waves.name: Wave 4: CI / Quality Gates
waves.objective: PRs run vet, test, lint, vuln, and real-age integration

waves.name: Wave 5: Runtime Security Hardening
waves.objective: Close the runtime threat-model items: age trust, identity hygiene, locks, audit, ops

waves.name: Wave 6: Web UI Hardening
waves.objective: Close web-surface items: cookies, host allowlist, cache, rotation, plaintext policy, scope

waves.name: Wave 7: Recipient Governance
waves.objective: Recipient adds gated by quorum; rotate flow on removal

hot.title: Makefile targets
hot.body:
Goal:
A `Makefile` with the standard set of targets means "every contributor runs
the same checks I run." Reproducibility is a trust signal.

Verification:
1. `make build` — local cross-platform builds.
2. `make test` — `go test ./...` race + coverage.
3. `make integration` — runs `//go:build integration` jobs (K-16) against real `age`.
4. `make lint` — `go vet`, `staticcheck`, `gosec`.
5. `make vuln` — `govulncheck`.
6. `make verify-release VERSION=…` — calls K-41's verify script.
7. `make help` — prints target descriptions. Acceptance:
8. All targets work on a clean checkout.
9. CI workflows from Wave 3 invoke `make` targets directly so local and CI stay in sync.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-47; wave/step 8.2; effort S; risk low
- Files: Makefile (new)
- Depends on: K-12, K-13, K-14, K-15, K-16
- Notes: CLAUDE.md prefers build targets over ad-hoc commands. This is the knot
that operationalizes that.

hot.title: Demo GIF / asciinema in README
hot.body:
Goal:
A 30-second demo showing init → recipient add → set → render moves adoption
more than docs do. Demos answer "what is this?" in seconds.

Verification:
1. Either an `asciinema` `.cast` file embedded via the asciinema-player shortcode, or a GIF (≤2 MB) under `assets/`.
2. README's first section after the logo embeds the demo.
3. The recording uses sample names that match the README's "Real-Life Flow" so a viewer can connect demo to docs.
4. No real recipients or identities visible in the recording; use throwaway ones generated for the recording.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-46; wave/step 8.1; effort S; risk low
- Files: assets/demo.cast or assets/demo.gif, README.md
- Depends on: K-04, K-05, K-09
- Notes: Keep it short. A 30-second demo gets watched; a 3-minute one does not.

hot.title: Reproducible-build verify target
hot.body:
Goal:
Document and prove that builds are deterministic. Provide a one-liner that
rebuilds locally and compares to the released checksum.

Verification:
1. `make verify-release VERSION=v0.1.0` (or equivalent) checks out the tag, rebuilds with the same `-trimpath -ldflags="-s -w"` flags and CGO/GOOS matrix, and diffs the resulting tarball SHA-256s against the published `checksums.txt`.
2. README "Verifying releases" section walks through the steps.
3. CI gains a "reproducibility" job (optional, slow) that runs the same verification monthly or on release.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-41; wave/step 7.4; effort M; risk low
- Files: Makefile, scripts/verify-release.sh, README.md
- Depends on: K-40
- Notes: Reproducible Go builds are mostly a function of `-trimpath` (already on),
matched Go version (`go-version-file`), and stable third-party deps
(already minimal). This knot is mostly about *demonstrating* the property,
not building it.

hot.title: install.sh: pin to tag, not main
hot.body:
Goal:
The README's quickstart points at
`raw.githubusercontent.com/cartine/thimble/main/scripts/install.sh`. Anyone
with push access to `main` can change the install script for everyone in
flight. Pin the URL.

Verification:
1. README quickstart's `curl … install.sh | sh` URL points at a tagged commit, e.g. `…/raw/v0.1.0/scripts/install.sh`.
2. The release workflow uploads `install.sh` itself as a release asset; the README also documents `curl …/releases/latest/download/install.sh | sh` as an alternative.
3. The script's first line prints its own SHA-256 (computed at build time) so an operator can spot-check.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-39; wave/step 7.2; effort S; risk med
- Files: scripts/install.sh, README.md
- Depends on: K-38
- Notes: Cheap. Do at the same time as K-38 to avoid two README-edit PRs.

hot.title: install.sh: mandatory checksum verification
hot.body:
Goal:
Today's [install.sh:34](scripts/install.sh:34) silently skips checksum
verification if the checksums file fails to download:

```sh
if curl -fsSL "$base/checksums.txt" -o "$tmp/checksums.txt"; then
  expected="..."
  ...
fi
```

A transient network blip — or an active MITM at the CDN edge that fails one
request and serves another — bypasses verification. This must be mandatory.

Verification:
1. `install.sh` aborts with a clear error if `checksums.txt` cannot be downloaded.
2. `install.sh` aborts if the checksum line for the asset is missing.
3. `install.sh` aborts on mismatch (already does, leave that path).
4. New flag `THIMBLE_INSTALL_NO_VERIFY=1` exists for emergency reinstalls but prints a multi-line warning before proceeding.
5. README "Install" section gains a one-line note that verification is mandatory by default.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-38; wave/step 7.1; effort S; risk high (current behavior is silently unsafe)
- Files: scripts/install.sh
- Notes: This is the highest-leverage fix in Wave 7. Land it first.

hot.title: Startup banner: scope warning
hot.body:
Goal:
Make the UI's intended scope explicit at startup so operators don't
mistakenly point a team or production deploy host at it.

Verification:
1. `thimble web` prints, before the URL line, a 3-line block: ``` Thimble web is a SINGLE-OPERATOR LOCAL TOOL. For shared/production workflows, use the CLI. Token rotates every 15m; press Ctrl+C to stop. ```
2. The first page of the UI shows the same banner in muted styling (it's already half-there with the "values stay redacted" pill — extend it).

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-35; wave/step 5.6; effort S; risk low
- Files: internal/web/, internal/cli/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: This is a UX/documentation knot, not a code-control one — but it lowers the
chance of misuse, which is itself a security control.

hot.title: Host-header allowlist (DNS rebinding defense)
hot.body:
Goal:
The loopback guard checks the listen address, not the incoming `Host` header.
A malicious page that DNS-rebinds a hostname to `127.0.0.1` would still hit
the server; only the 256-bit token saves you. Add explicit Host validation.

Verification:
1. Middleware rejects requests whose `Host` header is not in the allowlist: `127.0.0.1[:port]`, `[::1][:port]`, `localhost[:port]` (plus any user-supplied address via `--addr`).
2. Rejection is `400 Bad Request` with body "host not allowed".
3. For non-loopback binds, allowlist defaults to the configured `--addr`'s host; a `--allow-host` flag lets the operator add more.
4. Tests: matching Host → 200; foreign Host → 400; case differences in `Host` handled.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-31; wave/step 5.2; effort S; risk med
- Files: internal/web/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: This closes the only remaining DNS-rebinding vector worth worrying about for
a local-only tool. Combined with K-30 (cookies + SameSite=Strict) it's
defense in depth, not single-control.

hot.title: `thimble --version` subcommand
hot.body:
Goal:
Operators can't tell what binary is on a host vs. what shipped a fix without
this. Bare-minimum operability.

Verification:

3. Local builds without ldflags print `dev` for version and the git short SHA if available, otherwise `unknown`.

hot.title: Timeout + cancellable context for `age` subprocess
hot.body:
Goal:
`exec.CommandContext(context.Background(), …)` never cancels. A stuck or
hostile `age` hangs the operator's CLI indefinitely — the only signal is
"this is taking forever."

Verification:
1. All `age` invocations use a timeout context: 10 s default, configurable via `THIMBLE_AGE_TIMEOUT` (in seconds).
2. Signal handling: SIGINT to Thimble cancels the child cleanly.
3. On timeout, error message is "age timed out after Ns; rerun with `THIMBLE_AGE_TIMEOUT=N` if your hardware is slow or the bundle is large."

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-26; wave/step 4.9; effort S; risk low
- Files: internal/age/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: 10 s is generous for typical bundles. Large bundles (tens of MB) might need
longer; the env var is the escape valve.

hot.title: Scanner buffer guard for huge values
hot.body:
Goal:
`bufio.NewScanner` defaults to a 64 KiB token. A secret value larger than
that fails to parse with `bufio.ErrTooLong` — message is unhelpful, and
operators have no idea their secret was silently truncated on the way in.

Verification:
1. Dotenv parser explicitly sets `scanner.Buffer` to a sensible max (e.g. 1 MiB).
2. Beyond that limit, parser returns "value on line N exceeds <limit> bytes; store it as a file or split it."
3. Test for: 64 KiB+1 value round-trips fine; 1 MiB+1 value fails with the clear message.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-25; wave/step 4.8; effort S; risk low
- Files: internal/dotenv/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: Don't go infinite — a truly enormous value pegs memory and is almost
certainly a mistake (e.g. someone piping a binary blob).

hot.title: `and-get --env` shell/docker guard
hot.body:
Goal:
`and-get --env` exposes the secret through the child's environment block.
SECURITY.md flags it. We can do better: refuse to use `--env` for shell-shaped
children that almost always re-export.

Verification:
1. When `--env` is set and the child command's basename is one of `sh`, `bash`, `zsh`, `fish`, `pwsh`, `cmd.exe`, `powershell`, abort with: "use stdin or `--allow-shell-env`; child shell will export the value."
2. Same for `docker run` / `podman run` without `--env-file=-` or `-e KEY` pointing only at this var.
3. `--allow-shell-env` flag exists for users who really mean it.
4. README "Safe Secret Entry" section gains a paragraph explaining when `--env` is and isn't safe.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-24; wave/step 4.7; effort S; risk med
- Files: internal/cli/, README.md
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: This is a soft guard: a determined user can always work around it. Goal is
to catch the muscle-memory `thimble and-get --env FOO bar prod KEY -- bash
-c '...'` pattern that is almost always wrong.

hot.title: Suppress producer stderr by default
hot.body:
Goal:
`runSecretProducer` mirrors child stderr to the parent
([cmd/thimble/main.go:884](cmd/thimble/main.go:884)). Producers that log to
stderr — and worse, ones running under `set -x` or with debug flags — leak
the secret straight to the operator's terminal.

Verification:
1. Default behavior: producer stderr is captured into a buffer used only for the error message on non-zero exit.
2. New `--show-stderr` flag opts back in to live mirroring for debugging.
3. On success, captured stderr is discarded.
4. On failure, captured stderr is run through `redact` (truncate to 240 chars) before being shown.
5. Test covers: producer that writes the secret to stderr does not leak it to the parent's stderr in the success case.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-23; wave/step 4.6; effort S; risk med
- Files: internal/cli/, README.md
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: The same logic should be applied to `runSecretConsumer` if it ever becomes
chatty; currently it inherits the parent's stderr by design (the child *is*
the user's command).

hot.title: Identity-file mode sanity check
hot.body:
Goal:
Refuse to use a `THIMBLE_AGE_IDENTITY` file that is group/world readable.
Catches the most common operator mistake on the most sensitive file Thimble
touches.

Verification:
1. Before invoking `age -i`, Thimble `Stat`s the identity file.
2. If `mode & 0o077 != 0`, abort with: "identity file <path> is mode 0NNN; expected 0600. Run `chmod 0600 <path>` and retry."
3. `--unsafe-allow-identity-mode` flag exists for environments where mode cannot be set (e.g. some Windows / WSL setups), and using it logs a warning to stderr.
4. Test covers: 0600 OK, 0640 rejected, flag overrides rejection.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-19; wave/step 4.2; effort S; risk med
- Files: internal/age/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: `age-keygen -o` defaults to 0600, so this is mostly defending against people
copying identities around with `cp`/`scp`/`mv` and dropping permissions.

hot.title: Dependabot / Renovate config
hot.body:
Goal:
Drop-in upstream-update bot. Visible "I keep this current" signal and a real
defense against stale-CVE drift.

Verification:
1. `.github/dependabot.yml` covers `gomod` (weekly) and `github-actions` (weekly).
2. Auto-merge is *not* enabled for `gomod`. (We want the gate, not the auto.)
3. A label policy exists so security updates land with `security` label.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-17; wave/step 3.5; effort S; risk low
- Files: .github/dependabot.yml (new)
- Notes: The current dep set is tiny (`golang.org/x/term`, `golang.org/x/sys`), so
churn will be low. The valuable part is the GitHub Actions update stream —
`actions/checkout`, `setup-go`, `softprops/action-gh-release` all need
periodic bumps.

hot.title: PR CI: staticcheck + gosec
hot.body:
Goal:
Layer static analysis on top of `go vet`. `staticcheck` catches Go-idiomatic
bugs; `gosec` catches security smells (path traversal, weak RNG, unsafe
shell, hardcoded creds).

Verification:
1. CI runs `staticcheck ./...` and `gosec ./...` and fails on any finding.
2. Initial pass may need an allowlist (e.g. `gosec` may flag the `exec.Command` for `age` shelling — document and exclude with a comment justifying).
3. Each suppression has a comment naming the reason and a knot ID if a fix is planned.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-15; wave/step 3.3; effort S; risk low
- Files: .github/workflows/ci.yml, .staticcheck.conf, .gosec.yaml (optional)
- Depends on: K-13
- Notes: Resist the urge to broadly suppress; prefer fixing the underlying smell.

hot.title: Pin README install example to a real tag
hot.body:
Goal:
The README's `THIMBLE_VERSION=v0.1.0` references a tag that does not exist in
this repo. Either ship `v0.1.0` (preferred) or use a `vX.Y.Z` placeholder.

Verification:
1. Either:
2. Tag `v0.1.0` exists, the release pipeline produced its artifacts, and the README example continues to use `v0.1.0`; OR
3. The README example uses `vX.Y.Z` with a sentence pointing readers at the "latest release" page.

hot.title: Add LICENSE
hot.body:
Goal:
Add an OSI-approved LICENSE at the repo root. Without one, any policy-aware
org will reject Thimble at procurement-review stage regardless of code
quality. Highest-leverage trust signal in the entire plan.

Verification:
1. `LICENSE` exists at repo root containing full Apache-2.0 or MIT text.
2. README "Install" section gains a one-line "Licensed under …" footer.
3. `go.mod` / `cmd/thimble/main.go` retain no license-conflicting dependencies (current deps are stdlib + `golang.org/x/term` — BSD; compatible with both).

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-05; wave/step 1.1; effort S; risk low
- Files: LICENSE (new)
- Notes: Default recommendation: Apache-2.0. It includes a patent grant, which matters
for anything that touches secrets handling or crypto wrapping.

hot.title: Distribution: Debian (.deb) repo
hot.body:
Goal:
Many deploy hosts are Debian/Ubuntu. `apt-get install thimble` from a
hosted apt repo (Cloudsmith, GitHub Pages with `apt-ftparchive`, or
PackageCloud) is the cleanest install path for those hosts.

Verification:
1. Release workflow produces signed `.deb` packages for amd64 and arm64.
2. An apt-compatible index is hosted (recommend GitHub Pages + `apt-ftparchive` for free).
3. README "Install" gains a Debian section: `curl … | sudo apt-key add -` (or signed-by trusted.gpg.d), `echo "deb …" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/thimble.list`, `sudo apt-get install thimble`.
4. The `.deb` post-install script does *not* generate identities; it only installs the binary.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-44; wave/step 7.7; effort L; risk low
- Files: .github/workflows/release.yml, scripts/build-deb.sh
- Depends on: K-40
- Notes: This is the heaviest distribution channel; consider it L not M because of
the GPG signing key management story. Land it last in Wave 7 if at all.

hot.title: `make tag-release` automation
hot.body:
Goal:
Releases-as-a-button. Today, cutting a release means pushing a tag and
hoping the workflow is healthy. Wrap that into one target that's safe to
run.

Verification:
1. `make tag-release VERSION=v0.1.1` does: 1. Refuses if working tree is dirty or branch != `main`. 2. Refuses if `CHANGELOG.md` does not have an entry for `VERSION`. 3. Tags and pushes. 4. Watches the release workflow via `gh run watch`. 5. After workflow success, downloads each artifact, verifies checksum against published `checksums.txt`, runs `gh attestation verify` (K-40). 6. Prints a single "ready" line with the release URL on success, or a diagnostic on failure.
2. Tests / dry runs: `--dry-run` flag prints the steps without acting.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-48; wave/step 8.3; effort M; risk med (touches the release pipeline)
- Files: Makefile, scripts/tag-release.sh
- Depends on: K-08, K-40, K-41, K-47
- Notes: This is the closing knot of the plan. After it lands, every step from
"I want to release" to "I can recommend operators upgrade" is reproducible
and observable.

hot.title: Distribution: Dockerfile + GHCR image
hot.body:
Goal:
A minimal multi-arch image so Thimble can run inside CI/CD pipelines and
ephemeral container environments without an install step.

Verification:
1. `Dockerfile` is multi-stage: build from `golang:1.25` with `-trimpath`, copy a static binary and a pinned `age` binary into a `scratch` (or `gcr.io/distroless/static`) final stage.
2. Release workflow builds `linux/amd64` and `linux/arm64`, pushes to `ghcr.io/cartine/thimble:vX.Y.Z` and `:latest`.
3. Image is signed via cosign keyless (K-40 toolchain).
4. README "Install" gains: `docker run --rm -v $PWD:/work ghcr.io/cartine/thimble:latest list <app> <env>`.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-45; wave/step 7.8; effort M; risk low
- Files: Dockerfile, .github/workflows/release.yml
- Depends on: K-40
- Notes: Bake the `age` binary into the image, with a verified SHA. This is the one
distribution channel where K-18's pinning story can be made hard guarantee.

hot.title: Release: sigstore attestation + GH attest
hot.body:
Goal:
Today the release workflow has `permissions: contents: write` and produces
unsigned tarballs. Add SLSA build provenance and (optionally) cosign
signatures so installers can verify provenance, not just checksum.

Verification:
1. Workflow gains `permissions: id-token: write, attestations: write, contents: write`.
2. A step runs `actions/attest-build-provenance@v1` for each tarball.
3. Optionally: a step runs `cosign sign-blob` keyless against each tarball and uploads `*.sig` and `*.bundle` to the release.
4. `install.sh` has a verification path: prefer `gh attestation verify` if `gh` is on PATH, fall back to `cosign verify-blob` if `cosign` is on PATH, fall back to checksum-only with a warning.
5. README "Install" section explains all three verification levels.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-40; wave/step 7.3; effort M; risk low (additive)
- Files: .github/workflows/release.yml, scripts/install.sh
- Notes: GitHub's attestation step is free and sufficient for "trust the build came
from this repo at this commit." Cosign adds the option to verify outside
the GitHub trust chain.

hot.title: `recipient remove --rotate` flow
hot.body:
Goal:
Removing a recipient does not invalidate plaintext or encrypted copies the
former peer already obtained. SECURITY.md acknowledges this. UX-wise, the
right move is to make rotation the easy default after a removal.

Verification:
1. `thimble recipient remove <app> <env> <recipient> --rotate` regenerates every value created via `provision` (those marked random in metadata) and prompts for replacements on each non-random value.
2. `--rotate-randoms-only` skips interactive prompts and only rotates the high-entropy generated keys.
3. A summary at the end lists keys rotated, keys skipped, and keys that still need attention out of band ("STRIPE_KEY: rotate via Stripe dashboard").
4. Tests: provisioned key gets a fresh value; user-supplied key prompts correctly; cancel mid-flow leaves manifest in a consistent state (uses K-21 locks).

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-37; wave/step 6.2; effort M; risk med
- Files: internal/cli/, internal/store/, internal/age/
- Depends on: K-36
- Notes: Mark a value as "provisioned" in the manifest when it's set via `provision`
output (or via `and-set` from `provision`). That metadata is what
`--rotate-randoms` uses; it never touches values whose origin we can't
infer.

`--rotate-randoms` uses; it never touches values whose origin we can't
infer.

1. `thimble recipient remove <app> <env> <recipient> --rotate` regenerates every value created via `provision` (those marked random in metadata) and prompts for replacements on each non-random value.
2. `--rotate-randoms-only` skips interactive prompts and only rotates the high-entropy generated keys.
3. A summary at the end lists keys rotated, keys skipped, and keys that still need attention out of band ("STRIPE_KEY: rotate via Stripe dashboard").
4. Tests: provisioned key gets a fresh value; user-supplied key prompts correctly; cancel mid-flow leaves manifest in a consistent state (uses K-21 locks).

hot.title: Quorum-signed recipient list
hot.body:
Goal:
Today, anyone with merge access to the consumer repo can add their own
recipient via a one-line manifest diff and unlock plaintext access to every
future write. The README's "verify out of band" rule is process, not
enforcement. Add a real gate.

Verification:
1. Optional `secrets/recipients.signed.toml` file lists current operators and required quorum (e.g. M-of-N).
2. `thimble recipient add <app> <env> <new>` requires either:
3. A signature file `secrets/.add-<sha>.sig` containing detached signatures (age signing or sigstore) from at least M of the N current operators, or
4. `--bootstrap` flag, valid only when the namespace has fewer than 2 recipients (initial setup).
5. `thimble recipient list <app> <env>` prints fingerprints + signature status.
6. Tests: bootstrap path; M-of-N satisfied; M-of-N short-by-one rejected; signature over wrong recipient rejected.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-36; wave/step 6.1; effort L; risk high
- Files: internal/store/, internal/recipient/, README.md, SECURITY.md
- Depends on: K-20, K-22
- Notes: This is the structural fix for the single biggest social-engineering risk in
the model. Don't let it happen by accident; require it to be a deliberate
group action.

hot.title: Plaintext-input policy on web UI
hot.body:
Goal:
The web UI's `<input type="password">` accepts plaintext in form bodies — a
direct contradiction of the otherwise-strict CLI rule of never accepting
values via path-visible mechanisms. Form bodies live in browser autofill,
page-cache, refresh-resubmit, DevTools network panel.

Verification:
1. **Strict mode (default)**: Web UI removes the value field entirely. The UI becomes purely a *redacted* viewer + recipient/key manager. To set a value, the operator must run a CLI command. The page shows the exact shell command to copy: `thimble set <app> <env> <KEY>`.
2. **Convenience mode**: Web UI keeps the value field but adds a clearly styled "low-risk only" banner, sets `autocomplete="off"` and `data-1p-ignore` (1Password), and never echoes the value back even on validation failure. Acceptance:
3. The choice is documented in README and SECURITY.md.
4. Tests confirm: in strict mode, POST to `/secret` with action `create` or `update` and a non-empty `value` returns 400 with a pointer at the CLI.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-34; wave/step 5.5; effort M; risk med
- Files: internal/web/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: Strong default recommendation: strict mode. The UI's job is "let me see
namespaces and manage recipients," not "type secrets into a textarea."

hot.title: Web token rotation (`--rotate-token`)
hot.body:
Goal:
Today's token lives for the lifetime of the server process and never
rotates. Add manual rotation, plus an idle-rotate timer.

Verification:
1. `thimble web rotate-token` (or a SIGUSR1 handler if simpler) regenerates the token, prints the new value to the controlling terminal, invalidates all existing sessions.
2. `--idle-rotate=15m` (default) rotates the token after N minutes of no authenticated requests; existing sessions then re-authenticate.
3. Tests cover: post-rotation, old cookies are 401; new token works; SIGUSR1 handling on platforms that support it.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-33; wave/step 5.4; effort S; risk low
- Files: internal/web/
- Depends on: K-30
- Notes: Idle rotation is the cheap win. Manual rotation is the operator's escape
hatch when they think a token may have leaked.

hot.title: Move web token from URL to cookie
hot.body:
Goal:
The startup line prints `http://127.0.0.1:8787/?token=…`. That URL ends up in
shell history, terminal scrollback, screen-share recordings, and (for
non-loopback binds) browser history. A cookie scoped properly fixes it.

Verification:
1. New flow: server prints the token at startup. The first visit to `/` without a session cookie shows a one-field form ("paste token to log in").
2. On submit, server compares with `subtle.ConstantTimeCompare`, sets a `thimble_session` cookie with `HttpOnly; Secure (when not loopback); SameSite=Strict; Path=/; Max-Age=3600`, and redirects to `/`.
3. All authenticated routes accept the cookie; the `?token=…` form is removed from links.
4. Logout link clears the cookie.
5. Tests: missing cookie → 401; wrong token → 401; correct token → 303 + Set-Cookie; subsequent requests with cookie → 200.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-30; wave/step 5.1; effort M; risk med
- Files: internal/web/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: For loopback-only binds, `Secure` is omitted (browsers reject it on HTTP).
For non-loopback binds (which already require `--token`), require HTTPS or
print a warning.

hot.title: `thimble doctor` subcommand
hot.body:
Goal:
A one-shot health/setup check that surfaces every common pitfall before the
operator hits it in production.

Verification:
1. `age` resolved path + version + SHA-256.
2. Identity file: presence, mode (0600 expected), readable.
3. Store directory: presence, mode (0700 expected), writeability.
4. Manifest: parseable, no version conflicts, all referenced bundles exist.
5. Web UI port (`127.0.0.1:8787` by default): availability.
6. Recipient list per namespace: count, format (after K-20), thumbprints. Output is tabular; non-zero exit on any failure. Acceptance includes a test that constructs a deliberately-broken setup and asserts each diagnostic fires.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-29; wave/step 4.12; effort M; risk low
- Files: internal/cli/, internal/doctor/
- Depends on: K-18, K-19, K-21, K-28
- Notes: This is the knot that's worth pointing CONTRIBUTING.md at: anyone setting
up locally runs `thimble doctor` and gets a list of fixes.

hot.title: Append-only audit log
hot.body:
Goal:
SECURITY.md acknowledges the gap: no audit log. Even a local, file-based
ledger of `{when, by, op, app, env, key}` would make incident response and
operator handoffs dramatically easier.

Verification:
1. `secrets/.thimble-audit.log` is appended on every mutating op (init, recipient add/remove, create, update, delete, set, and-set).
2. Each entry: timestamp (UTC), operator identifier (recipient thumbprint of the identity used to decrypt this session — stable, non-secret), op, app, env, key (or recipient).
3. File mode 0640.
4. `thimble audit <app> <env>` subcommand pretty-prints the log filtered by namespace.
5. Audit writes never block on IO failure; on failure they emit a stderr warning but do not abort the user's mutation.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-27; wave/step 4.10; effort M; risk low
- Files: internal/store/, internal/audit/, README.md
- Depends on: K-21
- Notes: Don't include values, ever. Don't include identity file paths. Thumbprints
are public — they can sit in git alongside encrypted bundles without harm.

hot.title: Bundle attestation (ciphertext SHA + signed manifest)
hot.body:
Goal:
A repo writer can swap `production.env.age` for an older bundle, or one
encrypted only to themselves, while leaving the manifest's recipient list
unchanged. Downstream peers won't notice. Bind the manifest to the
ciphertext.

Verification:
1. Manifest stores `bundle_sha256` for each `envManifest`. On decrypt/render, Thimble recomputes and rejects if mismatched.
2. Optional: manifest is signed by one operator's age identity (or a sigstore signature) covering all `bundle_sha256`s and the recipient list. Verified before any write proceeds.
3. `thimble verify <app> <env>` subcommand prints the bundle SHA, the manifest signature status, and the recipient list with thumbprints.
4. README "Peer Safety Rules" gains a bullet about reviewing `bundle_sha256` changes alongside ciphertext changes.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-22; wave/step 4.5; effort L; risk med
- Files: internal/store/, internal/age/, README.md
- Depends on: K-21
- Notes: Phase this: ship the SHA-in-manifest first (low effort, big win). Add the
signed manifest later — that piece is L on its own and depends on a key
management story we don't yet have.

hot.title: Manifest version + flock (TOCTOU fix)
hot.body:
Goal:
`rewriteEnv` does load → decrypt → mutate → encrypt → save with no flock or
version-check on save. Two operators editing different keys in the same
namespace concurrently silently lose one set of edits.

Verification:
1. Each `envManifest` carries a monotonic `Version` (or per-namespace ETag).
2. `saveManifest` reads the on-disk manifest under an exclusive `flock` of `secrets/thimble.json`, checks the version matches the one this command loaded, and refuses with a clear "another writer changed <app>/<env>; rerun" if not.
3. Reads (`list`, `render`) take a shared lock.
4. Tests cover: two goroutines mutating different keys → second one fails cleanly; same key → second one fails cleanly; lock released on panic.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-21; wave/step 4.4; effort M; risk med
- Files: internal/store/
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: `flock` semantics on macOS/Linux are slightly different but `golang.org/x/sys`
covers both. Don't bring in a third-party lock library — keep deps minimal.

hot.title: Strict recipient format validation
hot.body:
Goal:
`validateRecipient` only checks for whitespace and emptiness. A typo
(`age0…` instead of `age1…`) is accepted at init/add time and only fails at
the next decrypt with a confusing `age` error — sometimes hours later.

Verification:
1. `validateRecipient` accepts only:
2. `age1` followed by a Bech32 charset of expected length (X25519 recipients).
3. SSH recipients beginning with `ssh-ed25519 ` or `ssh-rsa ` followed by a base64 blob and optional comment.
4. Anything else fails with a precise message: "expected `age1…` or `ssh-ed25519 …`/`ssh-rsa …`; got `<truncated input>`."
5. Tests cover: valid age1, valid ssh-ed25519, malformed age0 (rejected), random string (rejected), trailing newline trimmed, leading dash rejected.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-20; wave/step 4.3; effort S; risk med
- Files: internal/age/, cmd/thimble/main_test.go
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: Tightening this also reduces the social-engineering surface on K-36's
recipient-quorum scheme: the simpler the format, the easier to eyeball in a
diff.

hot.title: Pin/verify `age` binary path
hot.body:
Goal:
`s.agePath = "age"` resolves through `$PATH`. A malicious binary earlier in
the path silently captures plaintext during encrypt and the identity-file
path during decrypt — every Thimble user's secrets, on that machine, with
zero indication.

Verification:
1. `--age-binary=/path/to/age` flag and `THIMBLE_AGE_BINARY` env var supported.
2. On startup, Thimble resolves the binary path via `exec.LookPath` and prints it once on `--verbose` or first-use of any decrypt/encrypt command.
3. Optional SHA-256 pin: `THIMBLE_AGE_SHA256` is checked before exec; mismatch aborts with a clear error.
4. README "Requirements" section is updated to call this out as a trust boundary; SECURITY.md threat model lists it as a residual risk if pinning is not used.
5. `thimble doctor` (K-29) reports the resolved path and SHA-256.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-18; wave/step 4.1; effort M; risk high (single biggest runtime trust gap)
- Files: internal/age/, internal/cli/, README.md, SECURITY.md
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: Don't make the SHA pin mandatory — that breaks installs across distros. Make
it loud-default and trivial to enable.

4. README "Requirements" section is updated to call this out as a trust boundary; SECURITY.md threat model lists it as a residual risk if pinning is not used.
5. `thimble doctor` (K-29) reports the resolved path and SHA-256.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-18; wave/step 4.1; effort M; risk high (single biggest runtime trust gap)
- Files: internal/age/, internal/cli/, README.md, SECURITY.md
- Depends on: K-12
- Notes: Don't make the SHA pin mandatory — that breaks installs across distros. Make
it loud-default and trivial to enable.

3. Optional SHA-256 pin: `THIMBLE_AGE_SHA256` is checked before exec; mismatch aborts with a clear error.

hot.title: Integration test against real `age` binary
hot.body:
Goal:
Today's tests substitute a sed-based fake `age` binary
([cmd/thimble/main_test.go:266](cmd/thimble/main_test.go:266)). That proves
arg-construction round-trips, not that the real protocol works. A real
integration test catches drift if `age` flags or output format change.

Verification:
1. A new integration test job in CI installs `age` (apt or release tarball), generates an identity, runs the full lifecycle: init → set → list → render → recipient add → recipient remove, and asserts the rendered plaintext matches what was set.
2. Tests are tagged `//go:build integration` so they don't slow the unit-test job.
3. Job runs on at least Ubuntu; ideally macOS too.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-16; wave/step 3.4; effort M; risk med
- Files: .github/workflows/ci.yml, internal/age/*_integration_test.go (new)
- Depends on: K-13
- Notes: This is the test that would have caught a hostile `age` swap at install time
— close to but not the same as K-18 (which addresses the runtime-side trust
boundary).

hot.title: Split main.go into packages
hot.body:
Goal:
`cmd/thimble/main.go` is 1,394 lines covering CLI parsing, store CRUD, age
shelling, dotenv parse/encode, web server, and HTML template. For a secrets
tool, auditability is a feature; a reviewer should land in a 200-line file
and answer "does this leak?" quickly.

Verification:
1. `cmd/thimble/main.go` — entrypoint only, < 100 lines, just dispatches to `internal/cli`.
2. `internal/cli/` — flag parsing, subcommand dispatch.
3. `internal/store/` — manifest + envManifest + atomic writes.
4. `internal/age/` — exec wrapper for `age` (encrypt, decrypt).
5. `internal/dotenv/` — parse, encode, quoting.
6. `internal/web/` — server, routes, template. Acceptance:
7. All packages respect K-01 size budgets.
8. All identifiers respect TAXONOMY.md (K-02/K-04).
9. `go test ./...` passes; behavior unchanged.
10. Public CLI surface unchanged.
11. Each package has a 1–3 sentence package doc comment explaining its trust boundary (e.g. "internal/age is the only package that handles plaintext outside of memory mapped within a single command").

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-12; wave/step 2.1; effort M; risk med
- Files: cmd/thimble/main.go (shrink to entrypoint), internal/*
- Depends on: K-01, K-04
- Notes: This is mechanical once Wave 0 is settled. The work is bigger if Wave 0 is
skipped — names and shapes will churn.

hot.title: Add Threat Model section to README
hot.body:
Goal:
Honest threat models build trust faster than feature lists. Make the
in-scope/out-of-scope split explicit so users can decide if Thimble fits
their risk model in 30 seconds.

Verification:
1. README gains a "Threat Model" section (one screen, not a paper) covering:
2. **In scope**: lost laptop with identity file, repo write-access attacker smuggling a recipient, network MITM during install, terminal history/scrollback exposure, accidental argv leak.
3. **Out of scope**: root on the deploy host (see thimble.md), `age` protocol break, malicious operator with a valid identity, side-channel attacks on the TTY.
4. Each in-scope item points at the control that mitigates it (recipient validation, atomic writes, no-argv-secrets, masked prompt, etc.).
5. Each out-of-scope item names the user's responsibility (e.g. "rotate after laptop loss; we cannot retroactively revoke past plaintext").

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-09; wave/step 1.5; effort S; risk low
- Files: README.md
- Notes: This section also unblocks K-06 (real SECURITY.md can link here) and K-07
(CONTRIBUTING.md can link here).

hot.title: Replace SECURITY.md with vuln-reporting policy
hot.body:
Goal:
The current `SECURITY.md` is internal review notes, not a disclosure policy.
GitHub auto-detects `SECURITY.md` and surfaces it in the Security tab — it
should answer "how do I report a vulnerability?" and "which versions are
supported?" Move the existing review checklist into `docs/security-review.md`.

Verification:
1. New `SECURITY.md` contains: (a) supported-versions table, (b) private reporting channel (e.g. GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting enabled, plus an email contact), (c) expected response SLA.
2. The "Security Agent Verdict: Approved" line is removed — it overclaims given the Residual Risks list.
3. Existing review/checklist content moves to `docs/security-review.md` with a date stamp and reviewer attribution.
4. GitHub Security tab now displays the new policy.

Constraints:
- Knot ref: K-06; wave/step 1.2; effort S; risk low
- Files: SECURITY.md (rewrite), docs/security-review.md (new)
- Notes: GitHub PVR (Private Vulnerability Reporting) is free and the lowest-friction
inbox; enabling it from repo Settings → Security takes 30 seconds.
